title: A local central limit theorem for triangles in a random graph
speaker: Swastik Kopparty
abstract: What is the distribution of the number of triangles in the random graph $G(n, 1/2)$? It was known for a long time that this distribution obeys a central limit theorem: from the point of view of large intervals (~ standard-deviation length), the distribution looks like a Gaussian random variable. We show that it even obeys a LOCAL central limit theorem: the distribution is pointwise close to a suitable discrete Gaussian random variable. Joint work with Justin Gilmer.

proof_stages.label: Problem statement and local limit theorem
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines the triangle count S_n in G(n,1/2), records its mean and variance and the known central limit theorem, then states the main pointwise discrete-Gaussian local limit theorem and explains why this is stronger than the usual CLT.

proof_stages.label: Fourier viewpoint and reduction to tail control
proof_stages.summary: After relating the theorem to earlier sparse-p and modular equidistribution results, the proof defines the normalized variable R_n and characteristic function psi_n(t), uses Fourier inversion to compare point probabilities with the Gaussian density, and reduces the local limit theorem to showing that the integral of |psi_n(t)| outside a fixed central interval is o(1).

proof_stages.label: Frequency decomposition of the characteristic-function tail
proof_stages.summary: The remaining task is organized as several overlapping estimates for different ranges of the frequency t, each proving enough cancellation in psi_n(t) to make its tail integral negligible.

proof_stages.label: Matching exposure estimate for midrange frequencies
proof_stages.summary: For roughly sqrt(n) << t << n, the edges in a fixed matching are left unrevealed so that, conditioned on all other edges, S_n becomes a constant plus a linear sum of independent Bernoulli variables with coefficients about n/4, yielding product cancellation of size about exp(-t^2/n).

proof_stages.label: Union-of-matchings extension to small noncentral frequencies
proof_stages.summary: To cover frequencies below sqrt(n) but above an arbitrary large constant, the matching argument is modified by leaving a union of matchings unrevealed and showing that the resulting quadratic part is negligible compared with the linear part.

proof_stages.label: Cauchy--Schwarz estimate for high frequencies
proof_stages.summary: For large t, the vertex set is split into V and an outside vertex u, the triangle count is written as tri(A)+<b⊗b,A>, and a Cauchy--Schwarz squaring removes tri(A), after which independence across Ω(n^2) coordinates gives decay about exp(-t^2/n^2) for t larger than n.

proof_stages.label: Filling the t≈n gap and assembling the proof
proof_stages.summary: A many-outside-vertices variant of the high-frequency argument fills the difficult range around t=n, and together with the matching-based estimates covers the whole Fourier interval needed for the tail bound and hence the local limit theorem.

examples.name: Classical iid integer-valued local limit theorem
examples.note: A sum of independent integer-valued variables is recalled only to make the term “local limit theorem” concrete before returning to dependent triangle counts.
examples.engagement_reason: he cites the classical iid integer-valued local limit theorem and states its pointwise Gaussian conclusion, but does not work through an iid example or proof

examples.name: Odd-versus-even triangle-count pathology
examples.note: The hypothetical fact that a CLT cannot distinguish a distribution supported only on odd values from one supported only on even values illustrates why pointwise or modular information is needed beyond a global CLT.
examples.engagement_reason: he invokes the odd-versus-even triangle-count scenario only as a pathology that ordinary CLTs would not detect, with no construction or calculation on it

passes.reason: The parity-supported odd/even triangle-count scenario is invoked as a pathology showing that ordinary CLT statements are insufficient for a local limit theorem.
A distribution confined to only odd or only even triangle counts is collapsed to one parity class and is invoked as a pathology showing CLT information alone can fail to give a local limit theorem.
A distribution forced onto one parity has collapsed mod-2 support and is used to show CLT-level information alone cannot imply the local limit theorem.
A distribution supported only on one parity class is a collapsed lattice case used to show that coarse CLTs alone cannot imply pointwise local behavior.
A hypothetical parity-supported lattice law collapses onto one coset and shows that ordinary CLTs cannot imply pointwise LLTs.
A distribution forced to one parity has collapsed mod-2 support and is invoked to show ordinary CLTs cannot imply a pointwise local theorem.
A hypothetical distribution supported only on one parity is used as an obstruction showing coarse CLTs cannot imply a pointwise local limit theorem.
The hypothetical all-odd or all-even support is a parity/lattice obstruction showing coarse CLTs would not imply a pointwise local limit theorem.
The hypothetical all-odd/all-even support is a parity-collapsed obstruction showing ordinary CLTs cannot imply a local limit theorem.
The all-odd/all-even parity scenario is invoked as an obstruction showing ordinary CLTs are insufficient, and its support is collapsed to one parity class.
The odd/even support scenario is invoked as an obstruction showing a CLT cannot imply a pointwise LLT, and its distribution is collapsed onto one parity class.
The parity-supported odd/even scenario is an adversarial obstruction showing a CLT alone cannot imply pointwise local behavior.

examples.name: High-frequency value t = pi sigma_n / 7
examples.note: The special frequency t=pi sigma_n/7 is used to illustrate that large values of t probe the distribution of the triangle count modulo a small integer, here modulo 7.
examples.engagement_reason: he takes the concrete high-frequency value t = pi sigma_n/7, rewrites the characteristic function as E exp(i pi S_n/7), relates it to triangles mod 7, and then runs the conditioning/Cauchy-Schwarz Fourier argument for that high-t regime
